structures.key_variations: I don't think; I think so; I thought (past).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Doubling negative (I think I don't like it. -> I don't think I like it).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I think it's a good idea.", "I don't think she's home.", "I think so."
examples: I think you're right.
I don't think it will rain.
I think it's time to go.

structures.primary_communicative_function: Casual way to say what you want.
structures.when_to_use: To suggest an activity or state.
structures.key_variations: I don't feel like; Do you feel like?
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, using with clause (I feel like I need... okay but different nuance).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I feel like pizza.", "I don't feel like going out.", "Do you feel like a walk?"
examples: I feel like a cup of coffee.
She feels like dancing.
I don't feel like working today.

structures.structure: I'm good at / bad at + noun/verb-ing
structures.key_variations: pretty good, terrible at, not bad at.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, forgetting -ing.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm good at sports.", "She's bad at keeping secrets.", "Are you good at drawing?"
examples: I'm good at cooking.
He's terrible at singing.
She's not very good at multitasking.

structures.core_meaning: Desire for someone else to act.
structures.when_to_use: Telling someone what you wish they'd do.
structures.key_variations: Would like you to; expect you to.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using that-clause (I want that you help), omitting to.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I want you to meet someone.", "She wants me to go.", "Do you want him to call?"
examples: I want you to listen carefully.
He wants me to help him.
They want us to arrive early.

structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about how long something requires.
structures.key_variations: It takes me 10 minutes; How long does it take?
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using for (It takes for me), wrong word order.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It takes 20 minutes to walk there.", "It takes me an hour to get ready.", "How long does it take to fly to Paris?"
examples: It takes about 15 minutes to cook.
It took her two hours to finish.
It doesn't take long to learn.

structures.structure: There is no point in + verb-ing
structures.key_variations: It's no use + verb-ing; What's the point of...?
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, forgetting in.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "There's no point in waiting.", "There's no point arguing.", "What's the point of trying?"
examples: There's no point in crying over spilt milk.
There's no point calling him now; he's asleep.
I think there's no point in worrying.

structures.key_variations: The more I practice, the better I get; The less... the less.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Missing "the", wrong word order.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "The more you work, the more you earn.", "The sooner, the better.", "The older I get, the wiser I become."
examples: The more you practice, the easier it becomes.
The faster you drive, the more fuel you use.
The louder she spoke, the less they listened.

structures.key_variations: It's worth it; Is it worth...?; worth + noun (It's worth a try).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, worth to visit -> worth visiting.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's worth visiting.", "It's not worth waiting.", "Is it worth the money?"
examples: The film is worth watching.
It's not worth getting angry.
It's worth a try.

structures.key_variations: I look forward to (formal); really looking forward.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to + base verb (I'm looking forward to see you), missing to.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm looking forward to the party.", "Looking forward to hearing from you.", "She's looking forward to meeting you."
examples: I'm looking forward to the weekend.
We're looking forward to working with you.
Are you looking forward to your trip?

structures.structure: I'm used to / I can't get used to + noun/verb-ing
structures.pattern: I'm used to + noun/verb-ing; I can't get used to + noun/verb-ing
structures.key_variations: Not used to; get used to.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using base verb after to.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm used to the heat.", "I can't get used to driving on the left.", "You'll get used to it."
examples: I'm not used to getting up early.
She can't get used to the silence.
He's still not used to his new job.

structures.key_variations: Due to + noun; Because of + noun.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using because and so together.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I left because I was tired.", "Since you're here, let's start.", "As it was raining, we stayed in."
examples: She's late because the traffic was bad.
Because I was hungry, I ate a sandwich.
As it's your birthday, I'll pay.

structures.when_to_use: When describing an extreme situation and its consequence.
structures.key_variations: Such + noun phrase + that.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using too ... that, mixing such and so.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It was so hot that we couldn't sleep.", "She ran so fast that I couldn't catch her."
examples: I was so tired that I fell asleep on the bus.
The music was so loud that my ears hurt.
He spoke so quickly that no one understood.

structures.primary_communicative_function: Explaining why something can't be done.
structures.when_to_use: When a quality exceeds a limit.
structures.key_variations: far too, much too.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using too much + adj, using "to" incorrectly.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's too heavy to lift.", "You're too young to drive.", "The coffee is too hot to drink."
examples: She's too sick to go out.
This box is too big to fit in the car.
He's too proud to apologize.

structures.primary_communicative_function: Saying quantity or quality is below the needed level.
structures.key_variations: enough ... for; enough ... to + verb.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Word order (enough big -> big enough).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I don't have enough time.", "She's not old enough.", "Is it warm enough to swim?"
examples: We haven't got enough money.
He isn't tall enough to reach.
There aren't enough chairs.

structures.structure: I have no idea + wh-clause / about
structures.key_variations: I've no idea; I have no clue.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using that, word order.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I have no idea what you're talking about.", "I have no idea where he went.", "I have no idea how to do that."
examples: I have no idea what time it is.
She has no idea who called.
I have no idea why he's angry.

structures.key_variations: I was wondering if... (more polite request).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using question word order after if.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I wonder if he'll come.", "I was wondering if you could help me.", "I wonder where she is."
examples: I wonder if it will rain.
I wonder what they're doing.
I was wondering if I could borrow your car.

structures.key_variations: Would it be possible to...; Is there any chance...?
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using can with Is it possible? unnecessarily.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "Is it possible to get a refund?", "Would it be possible to change the date?", "Is it possible to park here?"
examples: Is it possible to speak to the manager?
Is it possible to check out late?
Would it be possible to have a window seat?

structures.structure: I'm sure / I'm not sure + clause
structures.pattern: I'm sure (that) + clause; I'm not sure if/whether/wh...
structures.key_variations: I'm positive; I doubt if.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using surely incorrectly.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm sure you'll love it.", "I'm not sure if he's coming.", "Are you sure about that?"
examples: I'm sure it's going to be fun.
I'm not sure where I left my keys.
He's not sure whether to go.

structures.primary_communicative_function: Softening a critical or disappointing statement.
structures.when_to_use: Before giving a negative or honest view.
structures.key_variations: To tell you the truth; Honestly; Frankly.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using it with positive obvious truths unnecessarily.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "To be honest, I didn't like it.", "To be honest, I'm not really interested.", "To be honest, I was a bit disappointed."
examples: To be honest, I think it's too expensive.
To be honest, I forgot about the meeting.
To be honest, I'm exhausted.

structures.key_variations: From my perspective; As I see it; Personally, I think.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using according to me (incorrect).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "In my opinion, it's a waste of time.", "In my opinion, we should wait."
examples: In my opinion, this is the best solution.
In my opinion, the movie was too long.
In my opinion, everyone should learn a language.

structures.when_to_use: News, reports, not for personal opinion.
structures.key_variations: Based on; As per (formal).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: According to me (wrong); using for opinion.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "According to the weather forecast, it will rain.", "According to John, the meeting is cancelled."
examples: According to the newspaper, prices are rising.
According to the recipe, we need three eggs.
According to him, everything is fine.

structures.key_variations: To the best of my knowledge; As far as I can tell.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using for definite facts.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "As far as I know, she still works there.", "As far as I know, it's free."
examples: As far as I know, the flight is on time.
As far as I know, he's single.
As far as I'm aware, no decision has been made.

structures.key_variations: The reason is that... (more formal).
structures.common_learner_mistakes: The reason is because... (redundant but accepted in spoken), using The reason why... is because... same.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "The reason why I'm late is because I missed the bus.", "The reason I called is because I need your help."
examples: The reason I'm tired is because I stayed up late.
The reason she quit is because of the stress.
The reason why we chose this hotel is because it's cheap.

structures.when_to_use: To stress that both are true.
structures.key_variations: ...but...also; Subject + not only + verb + but also...
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting inversion when "Not only" starts a sentence.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "Not only is she smart, but she's also kind.", "He not only plays the guitar but also sings."
examples: Not only did we arrive late, but we also forgot the tickets.
She's not only a teacher but also a writer.
The trip was not only fun but also educational.

structures.structure: The first time I + past simple
structures.pattern: The first time I + [past verb phrase], + [past result]
structures.key_variations: The first time I saw/met/went.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "The first time I saw her, I knew...", "The first time I went abroad was in 2010."
examples: The first time I ate sushi, I didn't like it.
The first time I drove a car, I was terrified.
The first time we met, we talked all night.

structures.core_meaning: Long time since something last happened.
structures.when_to_use: When you haven't done something for a long time.
structures.pattern: It's + been + ages / a long time + since + [past simple]
structures.key_variations: It's years since I saw him; It's been a while.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present perfect after since.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's been ages since we last spoke.", "It's years since I went to the cinema."
examples: It's been ages since I had a proper holiday.
It's a long time since I felt this happy.
It's been ages since I saw you!

structures.when_to_use: To admit something you can't control.
structures.key_variations: She couldn't help laughing; I can't help it.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to verb, or I can't help but + base (both possible but verb-ing is simpler).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I can't help feeling anxious.", "I can't help laughing.", "I couldn't help overhearing."
examples: I can't help thinking about him.
She can't help eating chocolate.
I couldn't help notice your accent.

structures.core_meaning: Emphasizing impossibility of a greater thing.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Highlighting contrast between simple and difficult.
structures.when_to_use: To say "A is impossible, so B is definitely impossible".
structures.key_variations: I can't even walk, let alone run.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using positive form, word order.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I can't afford a bike, let alone a car.", "He can't boil an egg, let alone cook a dinner."
examples: She can't read, let alone write.
I couldn't remember her name, let alone her phone number.
He doesn't have a job, let alone a career.

structures.structure: I find it + adjective + to + verb
structures.core_meaning: Expressing personal experience of an action.
structures.primary_communicative_function: Giving opinion about difficulty or pleasure.
structures.key_variations: I find it hard to believe; I find it easy.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using that clause without it (I find that it is... possible but chunk differs).
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I find it hard to concentrate.", "She finds it easy to make friends.", "Do you find it difficult to say no?"
examples: I find it difficult to wake up early.
He finds it boring to sit in meetings.
They find it impossible to agree.

structures.structure: I'd prefer it if + subject + past simple
structures.primary_communicative_function: Making soft requests for different behavior.
structures.when_to_use: When you want someone to do something differently.
structures.key_variations: I'd rather you + past simple.
structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present or will after if.
structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'd prefer it if you didn't smoke.", "I'd prefer it if we left early.", "I'd prefer it if you called first."
examples: I'd prefer it if you parked somewhere else.
She'd prefer it if he didn't talk about politics.
I'd prefer it if we met tomorrow instead.
